name: Cala Bóquer via the Portell de Sementer — circular from the Port de Pollença
name_original: Cala Bóquer por el Portell de Sementer — circular desde el Port de Pollença
region_intro: Hiking around Pollença offers diverse landscapes, situated at the foot of the Serra de Tramuntana mountains, a UNESCO World Heritage site. The region features a rugged northern coast with numerous cliffs and hidden coves, alongside valleys and varied ecosystems including holm oak groves and garrigue. Trails traverse mountainous terrain, coastal paths, and areas with significant natural features. Best hiking trails around Pollença The most popular hiking route is Cala Bóquer via the Portell de Sementer — circular from the Port de Pollença, a 3.2 miles (5.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 36 minutes to complete. This moderate route leads to an enchanting cove with clear waters. Another top favourite among local hikers is View of Pollença – Calvari Steps loop from Pollença, a moderate 4.4 miles (7.0 km) path. This trail includes the 365 Calvari Steps, offering views over the town and surrounding area. Local hikers also love the View of Cala Sant Vicenç Bay – View of Punta Galera loop from Cala Sant Vicenç, a 6.0 miles (9.7 km) trail leading through coastal scenery, often completed in about 3 hours 25 minutes. Hiking around Pollença is defined by the Serra de Tramuntana mountains, rugged coastal paths, and scenic valleys. The network offers options for various ability levels, from gentle walks to more challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Pollença are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 5,200 reviews. More than 31,000 hikers have used komoot to explore Pollença's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Pollença?
A: Pollença offers a wide variety of hiking experiences, with over 230 routes available. These range from easy strolls to more challenging mountain ascents, catering to different fitness levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in Pollença?
A: The terrain around Pollença is incredibly diverse. You'll find trails traversing the majestic Serra de Tramuntana mountains, a UNESCO World Heritage site, offering impressive alpine scenery. There are also rugged coastal paths with cliffs and hidden coves, as well as routes through valleys, holm oak groves, and garrigue. Expect a mix of rocky paths, dirt trails, and some paved sections, especially near towns.

Q: Are there any family-friendly hiking options in Pollença?
A: Yes, Pollença has several trails suitable for families. An excellent option is the hike to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999016555/cala-boquer-via-the-portell-de-sementer-circular-from-the-port-de-pollenca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cala Bóquer&lt;/a&gt;, which is an easy, approximately two-hour round trip leading to an enchanting cove. Another gentler option is the hike to Puig de Santuïri, offering great views and a quieter, rural experience.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or viewpoints I can reach on a hike?
A: Many hikes in Pollença lead to stunning landmarks and viewpoints. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24546070" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Pollença – Calvari Steps loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you up 365 steps to a hilltop chapel, providing magnificent views over the town. The Puig de Maria, topped by a 14th-century sanctuary, also offers panoramic vistas of the bays of Pollença and Alcúdia. For breathtaking views of Formentor, consider the Na Blanca trail or the Talaia d'Albercutx, an old medieval watchtower.

Q: Are there any circular hiking routes around Pollença?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes to enjoy. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999016555/cala-boquer-via-the-portell-de-sementer-circular-from-the-port-de-pollenca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cala Bóquer via the Portell de Sementer&lt;/a&gt; is a popular circular trail. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24546070" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Pollença – Calvari Steps loop&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a moderate circular path through the town and surrounding area.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Pollença?
A: The mild Mediterranean temperatures make Pollença suitable for hiking year-round. However, autumn is particularly recommended for exploring the trails, as the weather is pleasant and the landscapes are vibrant. Spring is also an excellent choice, with blooming wildflowers and comfortable temperatures.

Q: Are there any caves or interesting geological features to explore near hiking trails?
A: Yes, the region has some fascinating geological features. Along the Coves Blanques route, you can find the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/184092" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Bunkers and Gun Emplacements at Coves Blanques&lt;/a&gt;. Other notable caves in the area include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/321817" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tancada Cave&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5583751" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sant Martí Cave&lt;/a&gt;, which can be incorporated into longer walks.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Pollença?
A: The hiking trails in Pollença are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 5,200 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse landscapes, from the dramatic Serra de Tramuntana mountains to the stunning coastal views and hidden coves. The variety of routes, catering to all abilities, is also a frequently highlighted positive aspect.

Q: Are there any trails that lead to swimming spots or coves?
A: Absolutely. Many coastal trails in Pollença lead to beautiful coves with clear waters perfect for a refreshing dip. The trail to &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999016555/cala-boquer-via-the-portell-de-sementer-circular-from-the-port-de-pollenca" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cala Bóquer&lt;/a&gt; is a prime example, culminating at a turquoise cove. The Coves Blanques coastal path also features small coves like Cala Carbó and Cala Molins, ideal for a swim after your hike.

Q: Is public transport available to reach trailheads in Pollença?
A: Pollença and Port de Pollença are well-connected by local bus services, which can take you to various starting points for hikes. For trails further afield in the Serra de Tramuntana, it's advisable to check specific bus routes and schedules, as some more remote trailheads might require a car or taxi.

Q: Are there any challenging hikes for experienced trekkers?
A: Yes, the Serra de Tramuntana mountains offer numerous challenges for experienced hikers. The GR221, also known as 'La Ruta de Pedra en Sec' (the Dry Stone Route), traverses these mountains, providing approximately 170 km of routes with significant elevation changes. The Torrent de Mortitx gorge also offers a more challenging route through northern Mallorca.

Q: Can I find trails that offer views of the entire bay?
A: Yes, several trails provide expansive views of the bays. The hike to Puig de Maria offers stunning views over both the Bay of Pollença and the Bay of Alcúdia. Similarly, the Talaia d'Albercutx, an old medieval watchtower, provides breathtaking panoramic views of the Bay of Pollença and Formentor.
tips: The "gate of the seed". The name indicates that agriculture was previously practiced in the Vall de Bóquer. Even if you can hardly believe that, if you walk through the valley today.

Pebble beach with crystal clear water, mainly used by local people such as goats. The Boc Balear walks unashamedly through the bathers here. 
The bay is also popular with yachts. There are practically no shady spots. 
Perhaps interesting for geologists, but for laypeople simply beautiful to look at: the fossilized sediment layers on the left edge of the bay.

The path is comfortable, it runs through the old agricultural terraces of the Vall de Bóquer at the foot of the imposing wall of the Serra del Cavall Bernat, fierce and inaccessible in appearance. The old agricultural plots are quite feral due to lack of exploitation and the autochthonous Mediterranean garrigue is colonizing the slopes of the coastal mountains, off the beaten track and peasant sidewalks it is difficult to walk. The wide and comfortable path ends at the extensive Coll del Moro, which has remains of buildings and dry stone walls. The views of Cala Bóquer are beautiful, a true pirate refuge in one of the last calcareous fangs of the Tramuntana before forming the rugged coastal ranges of Formentor.
